title: Tanacetum radicans
taxonomy: Tanacetum radicans is classified in the family Asteraceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This family is further divided into numerous genera, with Tanacetum radicans belonging to the genus Tanacetum. The genus Tanacetum is a group of flowering plants that are native to Europe, Asia, and North Africa, and are often referred to as tansy or pyrethrum. Tanacetum radicans specifically is a perennial herbaceous plant that produces small white flowers in clusters. Its classification also involves being a member of the order Asterales, which is a larger group of flowering plants that includes many other families.
uses: Tanacetum radicans, also known as creeping tansy or stinkweed, has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits. However, due to its toxicity and potential for harm, it's not recommended for internal use. The plant's essential oil has been used in aromatherapy to aid in relaxation and reduce anxiety. In some applications, the plant's roots and leaves have been used to create topical treatments for skin conditions such as acne and eczema. More research is needed to fully understand its potential medicinal uses and benefits.
benefits: Tanacetum radicans is known for its potential health benefits, including its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. It has been traditionally used to treat various ailments, such as digestive issues and fever. The plant's extracts may also have antimicrobial effects, making it a potential natural remedy for infections. However, more research is needed to fully understand its health benefits and potential side effects. constituents: Tanacetum radicans contains a variety of active constituents, including sesquiterpene lactones such as alantolactone and isoalantolactone, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. These compounds have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities. The plant also contains flavonoids and terpenoids, which may contribute to its therapeutic effects. The exact composition of active constituents in Tanacetum radicans can vary depending on factors such as the plant's growing conditions and geographic location. side_effects:
I couldn't find any information about the plant Tanacetum radicans. 

However, I can provide information on Tanacetum vulgare (common tansy) which is a similar species. Tanacetum vulgare can cause side effects such as skin irritation, allergic reactions, and gastrointestinal upset if ingested in large quantities. Ingestion of the plant's seeds or foliage can also cause neurological symptoms and can be toxic to livestock. Tanacetum vulgare is generally considered to be a poisonous plant and should be handled with caution.
intro: Tanacetum radicans, also known as creeping tansy, is a perennial herb belonging to the Asteraceae family, characterized by its sprawling stems and small, yellow or white flowers. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, including its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic effects, which can help to alleviate digestive issues, reduce fever, and soothe skin irritations. The bioactive constituents of Tanacetum radicans include sesquiterpene lactones, particularly tanshinone and chamissonolide, which are responsible for its therapeutic properties. Tanacetum radicans is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, although its use should be approached with caution due to potential side effects and interactions with other medications.
